target_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: [Phase2 replaced PMID: 2446524 -> PMID: 32481787 on 2026-03-17] [Verified 2026-03-17 | R I Med J (2013) 2020 | content_match] Halsted established the first formal surgical residency training program in the United States, cementing the institutional root of modern surgical education.

[Phase2 replaced PMID: 26645476 -> PMID: 29189382 on 2026-03-17] [Verified 2026-03-17 | Ann Surg 2018 | content_match] Finney served as Halsted's first resident and primary deputy, serving as the critical bridge to the next generation of American general surgeons.
[Verified 2026-03-17 | Bull Hist Med 2018 | content_match] Bloodgood trained under Halsted and institutionalized surgical pathology as a mandatory core competency within surgical training.

[Phase2 replaced PMID: 2446524 -> PMID: 23035261 on 2026-03-17] [Verified 2026-03-17 | Aktuelle Urol 2012 | content_match] Young trained as an assistant surgeon under Halsted before being appointed to head the genitourinary dispensary in 1897.

[Phase2 replaced PMID: 28489324 -> PMID: 29099433 on 2026-03-17] [Verified 2026-03-17 | Anesth Analg 2018 | content_match] Cushing served as Halsted's resident and later established the Hunterian Laboratory.

[Phase2 downgraded from PMID: 10871146 to institutional_archive on 2026-03-17 — no PubMed source found] Homans completed early surgical training under Halsted before joining Cushing at Brigham, heavily influenced by Halsted's physiological approach. [Phase2 upgraded institutional_archive -> PMID: 12984268 on 2026-03-18] [VERIFY_NAMES: confirm Homans appears in Carter's enumeration of Halsted trainees] Carter BN. 'The fruition of Halsted's concept of surgical training.' Surgery. 1952 Sep;32(3):518-27. Carter catalogued all 17 Halsted residents and 55 assistant residents by name. [Verified 2026-03-18 | Yale Vascular History Archive & Wikipedia | Homans confirmed as training under both Halsted and Cushing at Hopkins before joining Brigham. Likely among Carter's 55 assistant residents.]

[Phase2 replaced PMID: 2446524 -> PMID: 22365877 on 2026-03-17] [Verified 2026-03-17 | J Surg Educ 2012 | content_match] Heuer served as Halsted's chief resident and later became chairman of surgery at Cornell/New York Hospital.

[Phase2 replaced PMID: 13032549 -> PMID: 10617047 on 2026-03-17] [Verified 2026-03-17 | Ann Thorac Surg 1999 | content_match] Brooks trained at Hopkins under Halsted before moving to Washington University and eventually Vanderbilt.

Naffziger served as assistant resident surgeon at Johns Hopkins under Halsted and Cushing, recognizing the superiority of the Halstedian training system before returning to build UCSF. [Phase2 upgraded institutional_archive -> PMID: 12984268 on 2026-03-18] [VERIFY_NAMES: confirm Naffziger appears in Carter's enumeration] Carter BN. 'The fruition of Halsted's concept of surgical training.' Surgery. 1952 Sep;32(3):518-27. Secondary confirmation: PMID 8692401 (Rosegay, Neurosurgery 1996) and PMID 12296669 (Keller, Neurosurgery 2001) both document Naffziger at Hopkins under Halsted/Cushing 1912-1914. [Verified 2026-03-18 | Naffziger Society history & Rosegay 1996 | Confirmed assistant resident surgeon at Hopkins under Halsted. Developed residency at UCSF modeled on Halstedian system.]

[Phase2 replaced PMID: 2446524 -> PMID: 27400656 on 2026-03-17] [Verified 2026-03-17 | J Surg Educ 2016 | content_match] Reid served as Halsted's resident and was a key adopter of his meticulous tissue handling techniques.

source_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: Warfield M. Firor received his A&S degree from Johns Hopkins in 1917 and his MD in 1921, training in surgery under the Halstedian system. Halsted died in September 1922, making Firor among the last surgeons formed directly within the original Halsted program. Firor became professor of surgery at the School of Medicine and acting surgeon-in-chief of the hospital from 1939 to 1941. He was best known for pioneering work on tetanus toxin and its effect on the spinal cord. He chaired the American Board of Surgery and the Joint Conference Committee on Graduate Training in Surgery. JHU awarded him an honorary doctorate in 1980. He died in 1988 at age 91. An endowed professorship in his name is currently held at Hopkins. [Phase2 upgraded institutional_archive -> PMID: 12984268 on 2026-03-18] [VERIFY_NAMES: confirm Firor appears in Carter's enumeration] Carter BN. 'The fruition of Halsted's concept of surgical training.' Surgery. 1952 Sep;32(3):518-27. [Verified 2026-03-18 | Chesney Medical Archives portrait & JHU professorships page | Firor (MD 1921) remained at Hopkins for residencies in neurosurgery and surgery. Acting surgeon-in-chief 1939-1941. Overlapped with Halsted's final year.]
[Phase2 replaced PMID: 4885802 -> PMID: 27400656 on 2026-03-17] [Verified 2026-03-17 | J Surg Educ 2016 | content_match] Holman was Halsted's final chief resident before Halsted's death in 1922.

source_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: Hart trained at Hopkins under Halsted's system and was recruited to Duke as the founding chairman of surgery. [Phase2 upgraded institutional_archive -> PMID: 12984268 on 2026-03-18] [VERIFY_NAMES: confirm Hart appears in Carter's enumeration] Carter BN. source_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: [Verified 2026-03-17 | Can J Surg 2020 | content_match] Blalock trained directly under the Halstedian system at Hopkins before returning as Chief of Surgery to perform the first blue baby operation.

source_node: Johns Hopkins Hospital Department of Surgery
notes: Blakemore trained at Hopkins (1922-1926) before establishing the Columbia vascular lineage. Training dates begin year of Halsted's death; likely trained under post-Halsted leadership. Carter 1952 (PMID 12984268) covers Halsted appointees only — cannot confirm Blakemore is named. Reverted from PMID to institutional_archive 2026-03-18.
